bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century: From the Unique MS. B.14.52 in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, Second Series, with Three Thirteenth-Century Hymns, EETS Original Series 53 (London: N. Trübner, 1873).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series
bibliographic.title: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century

entities.description: Morris's EETS edition presents thirty-four Early Middle English homilies with facing Modern English translations, followed by A Moral Ode, notes, and a separate appendix of three hymns with musical and photolithographic material. The aha2705 subject is only the thirty-four source-language homilies and A Moral Ode.
entities.label: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (1873)
notes: The title page calls the hymn source MS. 54 D.4.14, while the appendix heading calls it MS. 54, D.5.14; these hymns are outside the aha2705 subject and the discrepancy is not silently resolved.
Morris's comparison notes cite other Poema Morale readings, but the exact route from earlier print to physical witnesses remains unresolved and is not modeled as direct codex consultation.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1873, and the official U-M item applies No Copyright–United States to this digitized book.
rights.component: 1873 edition text, translations, notes, and arrangement

entities.description: The principal and directly transcribed manuscript witness. Poema Morale occupies ff. 2r–9v; the homilies occupy ff. 10r–86r and, after lexical notes on f. 86v and a blank f. 87r, ff. 87v–88r. The codex is materially and scribally composite, so institutional and scholarly date/origin statements are retained at the witness layer rather than transferred to all component works.

next_steps: Inspect every comparison siglum and source acknowledgment in Morris's notes and preface.
Compare readings against Morris 1868, Furnivall 1862, Morris 1872, and surviving manuscript images where available.
Add direct collated_against edges only when physical consultation or an exact print route is evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Did Morris inspect the Egerton, Lambeth, and Jesus Poema Morale codices directly for the comparison readings in the Second Series, or did he carry those readings through his own and others' earlier printed editions?

next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and page-image inventory from U-M.
Confirm whether the repository artifact is byte-identical to an archived U-M XML export or reflects later transformations.
Continue to treat the file's explicit no-corrections declaration as the strongest item-specific production evidence.
openQuestions.question: What exact source-image package, OCR engine configuration, correction history, and later transformation history produced U-M item AHA2705.0001.001 and the repository XML?

next_steps: Check the current Corpus Christi College catalog and Morris's errata or correspondence.
Keep the three hymns outside aha2705 and preserve both printed forms until the discrepancy is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Why does Morris's title page identify the separately appended hymn source as MS. 54 D.4.14 while the appendix heading reads MS. 54, D.5.14?

next_steps: Compare the current web transcription against James's printed catalogue entry and every manuscript rubric.
Do not reduce the target count: the edition, XML, and physical loci support thirty-four prose homilies plus A Moral Ode.
openQuestions.question: How should the current Trinity page's abbreviated homily list, which appears to omit Morris's XVIII while still describing the manuscript as printed by Morris, be reconciled with the edition and XML's thirty-four homilies?

reviewNotes: The medieval component works, Trinity witness, Morris edition, U-M OCR item, repository XML, Internet Archive scan, and Trinity images are separate entities with separate dates, responsibilities, access paths, and rights records.
The cataloged subject is an edition-defined source-language selection: thirty-four prose homilies plus one verse item. It is not the complete 1873 volume and not a single medieval work.

Morris moves Poema Morale from the beginning of the manuscript to item XXXV after the homilies. The transcribes relation therefore records selected loci and reordered presentation rather than implying identical sequence.

The complete bound PDF and complete Trinity bundle both physically contain the target; each local copy explicitly declares target_work_presence present and maps the narrower work portion inside the whole cached object.
The 710-page PDF is a single carrier containing both series. Reusing facsimile:ia:morris-1868 preserves object identity even though this record's target lies in the Second Series component.

The Trinity bundle contains every one of the provider's 216 published canvases, including covers, flyleaves, two stubs and ten UV alternates. No spine or edge views are exposed, so complete means complete published inventory rather than exhaustive physical-object photography.

Morris's comparison readings are recorded as edition-level collation practice only. No Egerton, Lambeth, or Jesus manuscript entity or direct derivation edge is introduced without evidence of the consultation route.
The U-M XML's omitted back matter wrapper begins with printed p. 218, which is still the final facing translation of homily XXXIV; actual editorial notes/back matter begin on printed p. 233.
The title-page D.4.14 and appendix D.5.14 hymn shelfmarks are preserved as a conflict. The appended hymns have no direct connection with the homilies and are outside the target selection.

The Trinity image license is CC BY-NC 4.0; the ignored cache is a research copy and does not authorize commercial use. Internet Archive metadata supplies no scan-layer rights or license field, so that layer remains unknown rather than inheriting the edition's public-domain status.

lineage.summary: aha2705 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan uncorrected OCR encoding derived from Richard Morris's 1873 Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series. The cataloged selection comprises the source-language text of thirty-four Early Middle English prose homilies on printed odd pages 3–219 and the continuous 400-line A Moral Ode on pp. 220–232. It omits the homilies' facing Modern English translations, front matter, notes and back matter, and the separate three-hymn appendix and musical material. Morris states that the selected medieval texts come from Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.52; the manuscript places Poema Morale first at ff. 2r–9v, followed by the homilies at ff. 10r–86r and 87v–88r, whereas Morris reorders the poem as item XXXV. A verified 710-page bound Internet Archive carrier preserves both the unrelated First Series and the complete Second Series, and a verified 216-canvas Trinity IIIF bundle preserves the complete provider-published manuscript inventory, including target UV alternates. The record separates textual, manuscript, editorial, encoding, repository, and image-rights layers; it does not promote Morris's comparison readings into direct manuscript derivation edges where consultation routes are unproved.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors
catalogSummary.date: Edition-defined selection compiled and first published in 1873; the component works and manuscript copies have distinct earlier histories
catalogSummary.region: England; Trinity MS B.14.52 is associated with southeastern England, but that witness localization is not assigned wholesale to the component works
catalogSummary.title: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (source-language selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (source-language selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Richard Morris's ordered selection as represented in aha2705: thirty-four anonymous Early Middle English prose homilies followed by the Trinity B.14.52 version of Poema Morale as item XXXV, A Moral Ode. It is neither one medieval work nor the complete 1873 book. The XML omits the facing Modern English translations to homilies I–XXXIV, front matter, notes and back matter, and the separately appended three hymns and musical material. The manuscript, edition, U-M OCR encoding, and repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.

assertion.notes: This dates the edition-defined selection, not composition of its medieval components.

dateStatements.display: Compiled for Morris's Second Series, published in 1873
First published in this ordered Second Series selection in 1873

extent.description: The target BODY contains 35 DIV2 containers: 34 prose homilies in 36 P elements and one LG containing exactly 400 L elements. The XML has 407 L elements globally because seven Latin Lectori lines on the title page fall outside the cataloged subject.

formStatements.description: Thirty-four prose homilies followed by one 400-line verse item.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's broad eng code and Morris's historical Old English title are not used as the normalized language classification.

scope.description: Rubrics, biblical lemmata, citations, and other embedded passages

notes: The BODY DIV1 label Middle English homilies 1-35 is structurally convenient but generically inaccurate: item XXXV is the verse work A Moral Ode, not a prose homily.
Morris's manuscript order differs from his edition order: Poema Morale precedes the homilies in Trinity B.14.52 but appears after them as XXXV in the edition.
The XML's omitted back matter wrapper begins with the facing translation on printed p. 218; actual editorial notes/back matter begin on printed p. 233.
No composition-place statement is made for the whole selection because southeastern manuscript production is witness evidence, not proof of a shared origin for all component works.

next_steps: Consult item-level homily scholarship and repertories against each incipit and explicit.
Retain the edition-defined selection boundary until component identities are evidenced individually.
openQuestions.question: Which modern work authorities, individual composition dates, and source traditions can be assigned to each of the thirty-four prose homilies without projecting the Trinity copy's date or Morris's edition label onto them?

notes: Anonymous authorship applies component by component and is not an attribution of the compilation to a single author.

assertion.notes: Morris reordered the manuscript's opening Poema Morale after the homilies as item XXXV and defined the published selection.

subjects.label: Sin, repentance, death, judgment, and salvation

summaries.value: A mixed-form edited selection of thirty-four Early Middle English prose homilies and the 400-line Trinity version of Poema Morale, arranged by Richard Morris as items I–XXXV in his 1873 Second Series.

aha2705 preserves the source-language texts only. It excludes the facing Modern English translations, front matter, editorial notes/back matter, and the unrelated three-hymn appendix and musical reproductions, while retaining title-page and omission-marker structure at the encoding layer.

assertion.notes: The qualifier identifies the actual selected source-language unit and is not part of Morris's historical title.

notes: The historical bibliographic wording Old English is retained; the selected vernacular is cataloged linguistically as Early Middle English.

scope.description: Historical title inherited from Morris's Second Series

work.dateDisplay: Edition-defined selection compiled and first published in 1873; the component works and manuscript copies have distinct earlier histories
work.displayTitle: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (source-language selection)
work.preferredTitle: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (source-language selection)

publication.embeddedTitle: Old English homilies of the twelfth century: from the unique ms. B. 14. 52. in the library of Trinity College, Cambridge: Second series, with three thirteenth century hymns from ms. 54 D.4.14 in Corpus Christi College / edited, with introduction translation, and notes by the Rev. R. Morris.

work.regionDisplay: England; Trinity MS B.14.52 is associated with southeastern England, but that witness localization is not assigned wholesale to the component works

subjects.label: Sin, repentance, death, judgment, and salvation

work.summary: A mixed-form edited selection of thirty-four Early Middle English prose homilies and the 400-line Trinity version of Poema Morale, arranged by Richard Morris as items I–XXXV in his 1873 Second Series.
